MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Oct-28-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Yosef Peretz, Esq. (415-732-3777; yperetz@peretzlaw.com) of Peretz & Associates for Plaintiff.
Alison Crane, Esq. (415-981-5411; acrane@bledsoelaw.com) of Bledsoe, Diestel, Treppa & Crane LLP for Defendant, The Brandeis School of San Francisco.
Tad Devlin, Esq. (415-926-7635; tdevlin@kdvlaw.com) of Kaufman Dolowich Voluck for Defendant, Lawrence Russo.

ARGUED The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant The Brandeis School of San Francisco's motion for summary judgment is denied.

Defendant's motion for summary adjudication on the second "cause of action" for negligence per se is denied. "[T]he doctrine of negligence per se is not a separate cause of action, but creates an evidentiary presumption that affects the standard of care in a cause of action for negligence." (Johnson v. Honeywell International Inc. (2009) 179 Cal.App.4th 549, 555.) Therefore, since the doctrine is not a cause of action, the court cannot summarily adjudicate the issue pursuant to CCP 437c(f)(1). Nevertheless, defendant correctly notes that the pleaded Education Code sections (32261 and 44807) only apply to public schools and defendant is a private school. (UMF 1). Pursuant to CCP 436, the court strikes the second "cause of action" as irrelevant and improper. Plaintiff's request for leave to amend to plead sections of the California Constitution is denied as procedurally improper.

Defendant's motion for summary adjudication is denied as to causes of action three through five. Defendant failed to maintain its burden of production. (See CCP 437c(p)(2).) Defendant's argument that it owed no duty of care because the alleged assault was unforeseeable founders. Plaintiff alleges, inter alia, that defendant failed to properly vet Mr. Russo before hiring him. (Amended Complaint, pars. 129, 149.) "Whether a duty exists is a question of law to be resolved by the court." (Doe v. Lawndale Elementary School Dist. (2021) 72 Cal. App. 5th 113, 125.) "A special relationship is formed between a school district and its students resulting in the imposition of an affirmative duty on the school district to take all reasonable steps to protect its students." (M.W. v. Panama Buena Vista Union School Dist. (2003) 110 Cal.App.4th 508, 517.) Plaintiff pupil and defendant school had a special relationship and defendant fails to show as a matter of law that it took reasonable measures to check Mr. Russo's background. (See Evan F. v. Hughson United Methodist Church (1992) 8 Cal.App.4th 828, 843 [employer can be liable where it fails to use reasonable care to discover employee's unfitness prior to hiring the employee].) In fact, plaintiff shows that: (1) defendant did not follow its established hiring process and failed to check Mr. Russo's references (Peretz Decl., Ex. 32; Arzt-Mor Depo., 115:20-24; 125:3-8) and (2) Mr. Russo was previously reprimanded for not properly controlling himself around a student. (Russo Depo., 68:5-21; 73:2-23.) The trier of fact needs to resolve whether defendant breached its duty of care.

Defendant's vicarious liability argument is off base since plaintiff does not plead the intentional torts (e.g. sexual battery) against defendant. Defendant's motion for summary adjudication on the ninth cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress is denied. Based on the above, defendant failed to shift the burden. Defendant's argument that the post-incident conduct was mere trifles is unpersuasive since that argument only addresses a part of plaintiff's claims and defendant does not completely dispose of a cause of action. (See CCP 437c(f)(1).)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-22-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Yosef Peretz (yperetz@peretzlaw.com) for Plaintiff.
Rachel N Rivers (rrivers@bledsoelaw.com) for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT THE BRANDEIS SCHOOL OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT:

Defendant The Brandeis School of San Francisco's demurrer to plaintiff's 8th cause of action for IIED is sustained without leave to amend. As previously stated in the court's order sustaining the demurrer as to the IIED claim, Brandeis' direct conduct (math class placement, failure to offer counseling, etc.) is not "outrageous" conduct as a matter of law. A viable claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress requires "outrageous conduct" on behalf of the defendant. (See Davidson v. City of Westminster (1982) 32 Cal.3d 197, 209.) For conduct to be outrageous, it must be "so extreme as to exceed all bounds of that usually tolerated in a civilized community." (Id.) "The tort calls for intentional, or at least reckless conduct - conduct intended to inflict injury or engaged in with the realization that injury will result." (Id. at 210.)

Plaintiff does not present new facts in the FAC that constitute outrageous conduct. The heart of the IIED allegations in the FAC surround Brandeis' actions after the incident with defendant Russo. (See FAC pars. 189, 190 [failure to support plaintiff, failure to provide adequate mental health care, failure to share details of the incident to teachers, and failure to make accommodations].) These allegations are insufficient as a matter of law to constitute outrageous conduct. Plaintiff alleges that Brandeis did not help her recovery, but instead ostracized and retaliated against her. (See FAC par. 4.) However, "[a] retaliatory motive alone is insufficient to sustain a claim for intentional infliction of emotional distress." (Light v. Department of Parks & Recreation (2017) 14 Cal.App.5th 75, 102; see also Cornell v. Berkeley Tennis Club (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 908, 946 [neither supervisor's "inappropriate" comments nor his official actions rose to the level of outrageous conduct sufficient to support claim of intentional infliction of emotional distress].) Plaintiff does not allege that Brandeis continued to employ defendant Russo; to the contrary, she alleges that he resigned or was forced to resign after the incident. (See FAC par. 47; see also Brown, 40 Cal.App.5th at 1109 [plaintiffs did not allege that coach continued to sexually abuse after his sexual abuse was disclosed to defendant].)

Counsel for Defendant to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendant The Brandeis School Of San Francisco To Strike Punitive Damages From Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Sep-22-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Yosef Peretz (yperetz@peretzlaw.com) for Plaintiff.
Rachel N Rivers (rrivers@bledsoelaw.com) for Defendant.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT THE BRANDEIS SCHOOL OF SAN FRANCISCO's Motion To Strike Punitive Damages From Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint:

Defendant The Brandeis School of San Francisco's motion to strike the claim for punitive damages for failure to comply with Code of Civ. Proc., section 425.14 is denied. First, Brandeis failed to raise the issue in its first motion to strike, and therefore is barred from doing so now. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 435.5(b) ["A party moving to strike a pleading that has been amended after a motion to strike an earlier version of the pleading was granted shall not move to strike any portion of the pleadings on grounds that could have been raised by a motion to strike as to the earlier version of the pleading."].) Second, Defendant fails to establish that the The Brandeis School is a religious corporation. (See Code Civ. Proc., sec. 425.14 [plaintiff must obtain a court order before seeking punitive damages against a "religious corporation."].) The court takes judicial notice of defendant's Restated Article of Incorporation and Certificate of Amendment of Articles of Incorporation. These two documents do not show that defendant is a religious corporation subject to the protections of section 425.14. Article 2 of the articles of incorporation states only that defendant is a "nonprofit benefit corporation . . . organized under the California Nonprofit Benefit Corporation Law for charitable purposes." (See Def. RJN, Exs. 1 & 2.) Further, it states the corporation "is organized exclusively for charitable and educational purposes." (Id.) Neither document establishes that the corporation is organized exclusively or primarily for religious purposes. (Cf. Little Co. of Mary Hosp. v. Superior Court (2008) 162 Cal.App.4th 261, 265-266 [statute applied to hospital that qualified as tax-exempt religious corporation].) Indeed, the legislation by which the statute was enacted originally applied to charitable organizations generally but was limited to religious corporations before it was enacted. (Rowe v. Superior Court (1993) 15 Cal.App.4th 1711, 1721-1722 [Church of Scientology].)

Thus, plaintiff's claim for punitive damages are adequately pled and may be awarded if defendant was aware of Russo's unfitness but hired or retained him anyway. (See Civ. Code section 3294(b) [employer may be liable for punitive damages if it had "advance knowledge of the unfitness of the employee and employed him or her with a conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others or authorized or ratified the wrongful conduct for which the damages are awarded"]; see CRST, Inc. v. Superior Court (2017) 11 Cal.App.5th 1255, 1262.)

Prevailing party to submit a proposed order that is verbatim with the Court's ruling.

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-10-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Yosef Peretz, Esq. for plaintiff
Alison Crane, Esq. for defendant

RULING: The tentative ruling on DEFENDANT THE BRANDEIS SCHOOL OF SAN FRANCISCO's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT was contested and argued. After arguments, the Court adopted its tentative ruling with modification.

Defendant The Brandeis School of San Francisco's demurrer to the first cause of action for negligence is sustained without leave to amend. First, the allegations of the claim are duplicative of plaintiffs' other viable negligence claims. (See Compl., 61 [cause of action one alleges "Brandeis breached its duties of care to Plaintiff by failing to supervise Russo"]; 83 [cause of action three alleges "Brandeis thus breached its duties of care to Plaintiff by failing to supervise his [Russo's] conduct."]; see also 57, 80, 89, 95.) Second, plaintiffs concede that they do not seek to hold defendant vicariously liable for Russo's intentional sexual assault/battery and merely seek to hold defendant liable for Russo's negligent conduct. (Opp., 1:16-19, 4:21-22, 5:12-14.) The first cause of action pleads intentional misconduct (i.e., Russo engaged in unwanted touching of a sexual nature and leered at plaintiff), not a negligent touching. This negligence claim is therefore inapposite to the pleaded facts of this case.

[See word minutes for the Court's complete ruling.]
